coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature phosphorescence based on chitosan carbon dots for trace water detection in organic solvents and anti-counterfeiting application
coredata.dc:creator: Ni, Yeyan
Zhou, Pingyan
Jiang, Qiwen
Zhang, Qi
Huang, Xingyu
Jing, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Trace water is known to be an impurity in organic solvents, having an adverse effect on the chemical reaction or environmental safety. Developing a green, facile and non-toxic method for detecting trace water in various organic solvents is still a challenge. Distinct form conventional fluorescent probes, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) test paper (TP) has aroused wide concern owing to its characteristic optical performance, including long afterglow and non-background interference. Herein, we fabricated a new kind of RTP-TP on the basis of chitosan carbon dots (CDs). The anchoring effect of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) promoted the restriction of vibration/rotation and hindered the non-irradiative quenching of CDs by forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds. However, water molecules could interrupt these hydrogen bonds and therefore the RTP phenomenon would be disappeared. Further, the RTP-TP was used to detect trace water in various organic solvents with high efficiency, excellent stability, and reliability. In addition, the CDs-PVA solution can be also used as anti-counterfeiting ink via various printing methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescence
Chitosan
Carbon dots
Hydrogen bonds
Trace water detection
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs-based RTP test paper (RTP-TP)
RTP-TP detection of trace water in organic solvents
Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) properties of the CDs-composites
Detection of trace water in organic solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs. (b) TEM image of CDs (inset: the particle size distribution of CDs). (c) XRD pattern of CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (e) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS of (f) C1s, (g) N1s, (h) O1s spectra of CDs.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum (UV-Abs), fluorescence emission (FL), and excitation (FLE) spectrum of the CDs in aqueous solutions (inset: photograph of the samples excited by daylight and a 365 UV lamp). (b) The FL emission spectra of the CDs water solution under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence decay spectrum of CDs aqueous solutions. (d) Emission spectra of CDs solutions at different pH values ranging from 3 to 11 under the best optimal excitation.

(a) Normalized fluorescence (FL) and phosphorescence (PL) emission spectra of CDs-PVA film of the low temperature (77 K). (b–c) PL and RTP decay spectra and the corresponding fitting curve of the CDs-PVA film. (d) The emission of the composite film (CDs-PVA, CDs-CMC, CDs-PAA, CDs-PAM, CDs-PEG) under the excitation with a UV lamp (365 nm, PL mode) and after the lamp has just been turned off (RTP mode). (e) The RTP quenching of the CDs-PVA ink on wood-free paper. (f) Proposed mechanism and energy-level diagram of CDs-PVA (Abs: absorption; IC: intersystem crossing; Vib.: vibration; Phos.: phosphorescence).

The dependence of phosphorescent intensity in anhydrous ethanol (a), THF (b), acetone (c), and DMSO (d) with different water content. The bottom-left inserts show the FL and PL photographs of RTP-TP in corresponding organic solvents under a UV lamp (365 nm) ON and OFF. The corresponding top-left insets show the linear relationship between P/P0 and water concentration. (All the experiment was measured in parallel three times.)

(a) The photostability of RTP-TP in ethanol, THF, acetone, and DMSO under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm, 12W), respectively. (b) The response of 10 pieces of TRP-TP to water in ethanol solvent without (black) and with (red) water present (4 v/v-%). (All the experiment was measured in parallel three times.).

Anti-counterfeiting and information protection applications of CDs: (a) and (b) Images of the CDs-PVA based ink as stamp-pad ink under UV lamp (365 nm) ON and OFF, used in Chinese traditional seal and screen printing; (c) Printing a QR code on commercial A4 paper, and the printed portrait and QR code under daylight, UV lamp (365 nm) ON and OFF, respectively; (d) Images of printing using CDs-based ink and an interference ink on commercial wood-free paper under daylight, 365 nm UV lamp ON and OFF, respectively.
Determination of water in real organic solvents samples.

head.ce:title: Room-temperature phosphorescence based on chitosan carbon dots for trace water detection in organic solvents and anti-counterfeiting application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources
College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace water is known to be an impurity in organic solvents, having an adverse effect on the chemical reaction or environmental safety. Developing a green, facile and non-toxic method for detecting trace water in various organic solvents is still a challenge. Distinct form conventional fluorescent probes, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) test paper (TP) has aroused wide concern owing to its characteristic optical performance, including long afterglow and non-background interference. Herein, we fabricated a new kind of RTP-TP on the basis of chitosan carbon dots (CDs). The anchoring effect of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) promoted the restriction of vibration/rotation and hindered the non-irradiative quenching of CDs by forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds. However, water molecules could interrupt these hydrogen bonds and therefore the RTP phenomenon would be disappeared. Further, the RTP-TP was used to detect trace water in various organic solvents with high efficiency, excellent stability, and reliability. In addition, the CDs-PVA solution can be also used as anti-counterfeiting ink via various printing methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is an essential component of life [1]. Whereas, the trace water in organic solvents, usually considered as impurity, has an adverse effect on the chemical reaction or environmental safety [2]. It is of great importance to detect and quantify water in organic solvents. Compared to traditional water detection methods (such as Karl Fischer titration [3], photometry [4,5] or electrochemistry [6]), photoluminescent detections have been considered as a convenient and reliable strategy due to its unique features, including easy fabrication, high sensitivity, rapid analysis, and real-time motoring [7–9]. At present, researchers mainly focus on the fluorescence (FL) detection of trace water. In comparison, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) can be a better candidate for optical sensing applications due to their attractive afterglow performance to eliminate the interference from fluorescent backgrounds and scattered light [10,11].

To date, efficient RTP materials are still limited to rare-earth phosphors and transition metal complexes [12,13], which are suffered from drawbacks of high cost, toxicity, cumbersome operation, and are visible under severe conditions [14]. To address these issues, mental-free organic materials have harvested attention. Novel photoluminescence carbon dots (CDs) have become a hot topic in RTP materials research owing to their numerous merits, such as wide source, low cost, non-toxic, superior optical properties, and easy afterglow activation [15]. Compared with traditional organic dyes, purely organic CDs have wider emission peaks and larger Storks shifts [16–18]. The CDs-based RTP materials can be fabricated by fixing them with other substrates. Deng et al. firstly observed phosphorescence from CDs in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix with a lifetime of 380 ms [19], and such a historic discovery opened a new world for RTP materials based on CDs. Liu et al. reported a new method for preparing polychromatic RTP materials (from blue to orange) in boric acid (BA) matrix [20], where the formation of glassy BA during heating can effectively protect the stimulated triplet state from non-radiative deactivation. They further patterned the polychromatic RTP materials, which provided a new idea for the field of anti-counterfeiting encryption. However, the organic precursors of those CDs are still mainly limited to small organic molecules or aromatic compounds whose toxicity cannot be ignored [21,22]. Therefore, exploring appropriate non-aromatic compounds as precursors to obtain RTP materials is highly demanded. Yet, RTP materials based on polysaccharides are not much explored.

Chitosan, a natural non-synthetic amino polysaccharide, is derived by the deacetylation of chitin as a derivative of seafood wastes [23–25]. Chitosan can be probably used as a precursor for the preparation of CDs since it is a renewable biomass source with abundant nitrogen content (∼7 wt%). The self-doped N elements would increase the surface-state defects of CDs and improve their PL property [26]. In addition, the polymer chains of chitosan are conducive to the construction of continuous graphite structure in as-prepared CDs [27]. Organic acids were always used as a solvent to dissolve chitosan to prepare CDs because of the special solubility of chitosan [28–30]. In the carbonization process, organic acids may participate in the formation of CDs [23], which makes it difficult to directly identify the luminescent center of chitosan CDs.

In the present study, ultrapure water was used as the only liquid phase reaction medium without any passivate agents to ensure that carbon and nitrogen elements of CDs were from chitosan. The mechanism studying indicates that the abundant CN/CO groups on the surface of CDs can increase the probability of ISC and arrest nonradiative transitions. Furthermore, a new RTP-TP for detecting trace water was constructed by embedding the CDs into the PVA matrix and simply coated on non-fluorescent filter paper. In addition, the multi-functional CDs-PVA solution can be applied as security ink in anti-counterfeiting.

Chitosan (deacetylation degree ∼85%) was purchased from SINOPHARM (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH values were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 1750 ± 50), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, 800 mPa s), polyacrylic acid (PAA), polyacrylamide (PAM), polyethylene glycol (PEG-6000), ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) are purchased from Macklin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was supplied by an ultra-purified water system (EU-LS-100TF, Nanjing, China). All organic solutions were of analytical-reagent grades and used with a dehydration purification to remove trace water for testing.

CDs are prepared from chitosan through ecofriendly hydrothermal carbonization [31]. Firstly, 1 g chitosan was ultrasonically dispersed in 15 mL ultrapure water. Then the suspension was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-equipped stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the brown carbon nanoparticle solution was obtained. The mixture was centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 10 min to dislodge large deposits and then filtered through a 0.22 μm PTFE filter to remove small particles. The brown supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 1000Da) for 24 h, with ultrapure water changed every 4 h to exclude unreacted small molecular. Finally, the light brown aqueous solution in the dialysis bag was collected and lyophilized after strict purification to obtain the brown powder named CDs.

A series of CD-polymer films are fabricated via a drop-coating method [29]. 30 mg of CDs powder was first dispersed in 40 mL PVA, CMC, PAA, PAM, and PEG solution (10 wt%) respectively by slightly stirring. After completely removing the bubbles in these mixtures, 4 g of each mixture was poured into a PTFE dish, followed by keeping it at 50 °C for 24 h in a vacuum oven to cast the composite film. The samples are named by CDs-PVA, CDs-CMC, CDs-PAA, CDs-PAM, and CDs-PEG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-based RTP test paper (RTP-TP)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-PVA solution was coated on the surface of the non-fluorescent filter paper with the coating rod. And then the filter paper was oven dried at 60 °C for 2 h and cut into small strips (2 cm*1 cm) called RTP-TP. The RTP lifetime of CDs-PVA solution coated on filter paper did not improve significantly after dry for more than 2 h (Fig. S1).
CDs-PVA was used as security ink for Chinese traditional gravure seal, screen printing and inkjet printing, respectively, with CDs-PEG as interference ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP-TP detection of trace water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Ethanol, THF, acetone, and DMSO was selected to test and verify the practical application of RTP-TP. An RTP-TP was immersed into ethanol (0–22% content of water), THF (0–18% content of water), acetone (0–55% content of water), and DMSO (0–55% content of water). The corresponding spectra data was obtained by a FLS1000 spectrofluorometer with a 450W Xenon lamp. A smartphone was used to take photos of RTP-TP testing in different organic solvents.

The size and morphology of CDs were investigated using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 UHR, Japan Electronics Co., Ltd). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 on a VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) using the KBr pellet technique. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was carried out by using an Ultima IV spectrometer (Rigaku, Japan) from 5 to 60° at the rate of 5°/s. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) analysis was performed on an AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Thermogravimetry analysis (TGA) was measured using a TGA209 F1 (NETZSCH, Germany). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was performed using a TU-1810PC (Beijing PUXI, China). Photoluminescent spectra and intensity were performed using an FS-5 instrument (Edinburgh, Germany), with a bandpass of 10 nm. Afterglow spectra and decay curves were conducted by an FLS1000 spectrofluorometer with a 450W Xenon lamp (Edinburgh, Germany).

CDs were synthesized from chitosan following the procedure in Fig. 1 a. The morphology and size distribution of CDs were characterized in detail by TEM. There was a large quantity of quasi-spherical structures without any obvious aggregation in Fig. 1b. Moreover, CDs have a narrow size distribution of 0.5–4 nm and the average diameter is about 2.13 nm analyzed by Image J software (Fig. 1b insert). Thanks to the small size of those monodisperse nanoparticles, the steric hindrance effect is more likely to be reduced, which is conducive to copolymerization during the preparation of fluorescent materials [32]. The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1c) shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.32°, which reveals an amorphous carbon phase in CDs and the plenty of O/N elements on the surface of CDs [33].

The chemical composition and functional groups of CDs were explored by FT-IR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 1d, the broadband around 3356−3245 cm−1 overlapped stretching vibrations of O–H indicates the presence of amino groups (-NH2) on the surface of CDs. The absorption around 2932−2875 cm−1 is related to the stretching and bending vibrations of –CH. Besides, the absorption bands display several characteristics of chitosan. The stretching vibration of CO/CN at 1664 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of C–N at 1397 cm−1, which correspond to the Amide Ⅰ and Ⅲ, respectively [34]. This can be ascribed to that more CO and CN were formed on the surface with the dehydration and carbonization of CDs during hydrothermal carbonization. The peak of Amide Ⅱ is overlapped by CC stretching vibration at 1598 cm−1. In addition, the sharp peak at 1397 cm−1 is ascribed to C–N bending vibration and the emerging peak at 1070 cm−1 was assigned to the C–H bending vibration [35]. The surface state and chemical composition of CDs are further corroborated by XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1e) reveals three typical peaks located at 285.5, 400.4, and 534.9 eV, assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The relative atomic concentration of C, N, O elements are determined to be 62.35%, 3.32%, and 34.33%, respectively, corresponding to the property of endogenous N-doping of chitosan. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, N1s, and O1s of as-prepared CDs was obtained in Fig. 1f–h. The C1s bands can be deconvoluted into three peaks, corresponding to CC/C–C (284.9 eV), C–O/C–N (285.9 eV), and CO/CN (288.0 eV), respectively. The N1s spectrum can be fitted into two peaks at 400.2 eV and 401.7 eV, which represent N1s states in C–N/CN and amino N. The peaks appeared at 530.6 eV and 532.8 eV were the binding energies of CO and C–O, suggesting that more CO are formed during the hydrothermal carbonation due to oxidation [36]. As displayed in Table S1, the elemental species in the nanostructure of CDs were also explored based on XPS analysis. Compared to most reported CDs so far, the CC/C–C showed low content, suggesting that a large number of carbon frameworks with carbon vacancy were formed in the carbogenic cores. In addition, there are many N- and O- functional groups on the surface of as-prepared CDs, which is favorable for the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds [37].

The optical properties of as-prepared CDs were characterized by UV–vis and FL measurements. The CDs aqueous solution exhibited a brown color under daylight, while the diluted CDs solutions display strong blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (365 nm). As shown in Fig. 2 a, two typical absorption peaks were related to different electron transition behaviors, at 270 nm and 299 nm relating to two kinds of electron transitions. The former peak centered at 270 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC band, suggesting the formation of conjugated skeletons. The latter emerged at 299 nm belongs to the n-π* transition of CO/CN moieties [31].

Excitation-dependent luminescence behavior is frequently manifested in fluorescent carbon materials. Significantly, the excitation-dependent emission behavior of as-prepared CDs was shown in Fig. 2b. The optimum excitation wavelength was 457 nm, and the corresponding emission wavelength was 533 nm. According to the FT-IR and XPS analysis, various functional groups decorated the surface of CDs and tend to introduce different energy levels, indicating multiple emissive traps or luminescent centers within the as-prepared CDs [38,39]. Importantly, more surface oxidation or their effective modification can lead to more surface defects, leading to the red-shifted emission behavior of FL emission [40–42]. The fluorescence decay trace (Fig. 2c) of CDs can be fitted via a bio-exponential function whose average lifetime is 6.71 ns. It can be fitted to two lifetimes of 1.24 ns (τ1) and 15.69 ns (τ2), ascribed to the different functional groups on the surface of CDs which induced some electronic transitions [43]. In addition, the CDs exhibit a quantum yield (QY) of 38% (Table S2).

For further understanding the possible luminescent center of as-prepared CDs, the FL intensity response to different pH values ranging from 3 to 11 of CDs solution was also investigated in Fig. 2d. There is not obvious quenching with the change of the pH value and the emission peak of as-prepared CDs do not shift to a shorter wavelength or longer wavelength. This phenomenon mentioned above provided evidence that the luminescent center of CDs was free from the effect of protonation and deprotonation as the pH changed within a wide range [44], demonstrating that the CO/CN groups could induce the formation of luminescent center on CDs surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) properties of the CDs-composites
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, we have confirmed that CO/CN is the luminescence center of CDs. There is no phosphorescence observed from CDs aqueous dispersion at room temperature (even under nitrogen or argon inert atmospheres). However, a bright afterglow emission can be visible to the naked eye from CDs aqueous dispersion at a low temperature (77 K) (Fig. S2), which implies that immobilization of the excited triplet species via hydrogen bonds dominantly determines the phosphorescence emission [45]. To verify the phosphorescent potential of as-prepared CDs, CDs were mixed with several polymers, such as PVA, CMC, PAA, PAM, and PEG. As shown in Fig. 3 d, while CDs dispersed in polymers emit blue light under the UV lamp excitation. Notably, an RTP phenomenon was observed from the film of PVA, CMC, PAA, and PAM, which could be easily recognized by the naked eye under ambient conditions. Taking the CDs-PVA composite as an example, the fluorescence (FL) and phosphorescence (PL) emission spectra at low temperature (77 K) under 365 nm excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. 3a. The Stokes shift between fluorescence and phosphorescence is 73 nm and the corresponding energy gap between the singlet and triplet levels is about 0.44 eV. Such a large energy level difference between singlet and triplet states forbids the revising intersystem crossing (RISC) [33], which implies the RTP phenomenon of CDs-PVA film.

Decay spectrum and the corresponding fitting curves were further investigated to explore the RTP characteristics of the CDs-PVA film in Fig. 3b and c. The fluorescence decay spectrum of CDs-PVA film (Fig. 3b) is different from that of CDs in aqueous solutions (Fig. 2c) while the phosphorescence decay spectrum of CDs-PVA (Fig. 3c) is similar to that. It is obvious that the fluorescence phenomenon of CDs-PVA film originates from CDs and the emission behavior is affected by PVA molecular, thus influencing the radiative transition and nonradiative relaxation [33]. The fluorescence and phosphorescent lifetimes of CDs-composites are listed in Table S3. RTP decay spectrum of CDs-PVA film (Fig. 3c) can be fitted via a bio-exponential function whose average lifetime is 0.2 s [46]. The afterglow decay curve represents a biexponential fitting curve consisting of 0.03 s (59.25%) and 0.45 s (40.75%), indicating that the CDs-PVA film occupied multiple decay channels [43]. That can be ascribed to the different functional groups on the surface of CDs which induced some electronic transitions [19]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the RTP phenomenon of those composite films can be observed while the CDs-PEG has no RTP phenomenon, which demonstrated that the –OH or –NH2 groups in chemical chains play an important role in the formation of RTP. Herein, the possessed sub-fluorophores of CDs (CO/CN) are speculated to be enhanced and rigidified by hydrogen bonds [47], immobilizing and restricting the vibration and rotation of the sub-fluorophores to realize the efficient radiative transition [48]. Subsequently, the –OH groups only appear at the ends of the molecular chain of PEG (Fig. S3), which is insufficient to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds with CDs.

The potential RTP mechanism of CDs-PVA can be portrayal as Fig. 3f. Firstly, the CO in acetyl groups and CO/CN formed during the hydrothermal carbonization process allow the efficient spin-orbit coupling, which is conducive to the ISC and more efficient generation of stimulated triplet states. Therefore, the energy gap between S1 and T1 was decreased and a part of the stimulated electrons can divert from S1 to T1 via intersystem crossing (ISC). Secondly, the CO/CN, –OH, and –NH on the surface of CDs introduce the construction of hydrogen bonds to polymers (except PEG, whose –OH groups only appear at the ends of the molecular chain). The hydrogen bonds formed between CDs and polymers can not only restrict the intramolecular vibration/rotation but also stabilize the triplet exciton states, which enables efficient RTP emission. Fig. 3e and Fig. S5 show that the photographs and emission spectra of the CDs-PVA solution coated on the filter paper when they are dry or damp. When sprayed by water, the blue FL emission was not affected while the green RTP emission was immediately quenched. Also, the RTP intensity of CDs-PVA film decreases significantly while fumigated by water vapor and recovers after drying. The interesting phenomenon can be ascribed to that the luminescence center was not destroyed and that the original hydrogen bonds between CDs and PVA were broken by H2O. The destruction of hydrogen bonds weakened the system rigidity and strengthen the intramolecular vibration/rotation. To better confirm the above inference, FT-IR spectroscopy and TGA are explored to illustrate the differences between the pure PVA film and CDs-PVA film. As shown in Fig. S4a, the pure PVA showed typical stretching vibration of O–H located at 3276 cm−1 while the O–H stretching vibration peak of CDs-PVA film shifted to 3262 cm−1. The blue shift in the FT-IR spectrum can be ascribed to the association/disassociation of hydrogen bonding, where the intramolecular hydrogen bonds of PVA disassociated and the additional hydrogen bonding interactions between PVA and CDs associated. However, the typical O–H stretching vibration of pure PEG and CDs-PVA film has no shift in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S4d), which indicates that there are no additional hydrogen bonding interactions between PEG and CDs associated. Another evidence for additional hydrogen bonds formed via interfacial interaction is that the Tg of CDs-PVA film is higher than that of pure PVA film in Figs. S4b–c. The fixation of CDs in the PVA matrix can promote their triplet emission (RTP). Furthermore, the RTP decays of CDs-PVA film immersed in ethanol with different water content (0, 4%, 8%, 12%) were also examined at room temperature (Fig. S6). Water was gradually added into ethanol while the corresponding RTP lifetime of CDs-PVA film became shorter. It can be ascribed to the destruction of intermolecular hydrogen bonds by H2O, which can prominently crush the stability of stimulated triplet states and raise the possibility of non-radiative transition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of trace water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Enlightened by the phenomenon in Fig. 3e and Fig. S5 that the PL of CDs-PVA composite was disappeared quickly while its FL was free from affection with the existence of water, we conjecture that the CDs-PVA composite is potential for trace water detection. Here, we constructed an RTP-TP by coating CDs-PVA solution on a fluorescent-free filter paper. Various amount of water was added to diverse organic solvents to fabricate different detection systems. The RTP intensity of the anhydrous organic solvents is defined as P0, and that of organic solvents with water is called P. The content of water was calculated by the quenching efficiency (P0/P). As a typical representative, the water detection of RTP-TP in ethanol was discussed in detail in Fig. 4 a. The photographs inserted in the bottom-left of Fig. 4a show that the RTP phenomenon was quenched gradually with the addition of water into ethanol while the FL phenomenon was not affected. Thus, the as-prepared RTP-TP has the potential as a water detection sensor for organic solvents. With the concentration of water increased from 0 to 22%, the maximum RTP intensity of RTP-TP was decreased. The relationship between RTP intensity and water content can be fit well with an exponential attenuation model. Interestingly, a nice linear relationship can be fitted between the quenching efficiency (P0/P) and water concentration within the range of 0–4%. The resulting linear equation is P0/P = 1.214–0.225C (R2 = 0.9953), in which C is the corresponding water content in ethanol (v/v-%). The limit of detection (LOD) can be calculated based on 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution (n = 5) and S is the slope of the regression line. By carefully determination, the as-prepared RTP-TP could be used for water quantification in ethanol with a sensitive detection limit of 0.013% (Table S4).

Furthermore, the ability of RTP-TP for monitoring trace water in different organic environments were also explored in Fig. 4bcd, including THF, acetone, and DMSO. The PL could be quenched efficiently while the FL kept glowing with the increase of the water content. The RTP intensities and the water content (THF: 0–18%, acetone: 0–55%, DMSO: 0–55%) can be accorded with an exponential attenuation model. Also, a nice linear regression model can be fitted between the quenching efficiency (P0/P) and water concentration (THF: 0–4%, acetone: 0–20%, DMSO: 0–6%), and the LODs were calculated by 0.026% in THF, 0.088% in acetone and 0.057% in DMSO, such a small LOD implies that the RTP-TP has high analytical sensitivity. Furthermore, the RTP-TP was still kept satisfactory stability, high precision and excellent repeatability (Fig. 5, Table 1) in different commercial organic solvents. Meanwhile, the water detection research of FL or PL sensor in different organic environments were listed in Table S4. The LODs of as-prepared RTP-TP are lower than other FL probe on the basis of biomass or organic compounds and the as-prepared RTP-TP reveals equivalent or better performance than certain metal particles.

In the era of the rapid development of information, anti-counterfeiting technology is of the greatest significance. In this work, as-prepared CDs-polymers were applied to optical security ink to realize the double anti-counterfeiting for its unique characteristics and particularity. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, CDs-PVA aqueous solution was used as ink for Chinese traditional gravure seal and screen printing, respectively. The anti-counterfeiting mark emitted obvious blue fluorescence when the pattern was irradiated by a UV lamp (365 nm), which was the same as the traditional optical anti-counterfeiting mark. However, the luminous color of the mark changed from blue to green due to the afterglow emission of CDs-PVA, while the UV lamp turned off. Besides, we also investigated its application in information protection. A QR code containing encrypted information was printed on commercial paper via inkjet printing (Fig. 6c). The QR code was colorless and invisible under daylight and it is difficult to recognize due to the strong background fluorescence of A4 paper while irradiated by a UV lamp. However, it emitted bright afterglow while the UV lamp turned off, it is easy for us to identify the encrypted information. On this basis, we designed another information encryption method with higher confidentiality, introducing an interference of fluorescent CDs-PEG. In Fig. 6d and e, the information “8888” was printed on the non-fluorescent filter paper, in which the right information “2021” was printed by CDs-PVA solution and the interferent information was printed by CDs-PEG solution. It is impossible to identify the printed number of “8888” under daylight, but the interference information of “8888” can be seen clearly under a UV lamp. It is incorrect information. CDs-PEG shows blue fluorescence but no RTP, while CDs-PVA shows blue fluorescence green RTP. As a result, the correct message of “2021” can be observed when the UV lamp is off. These observations indicate that the CDs-PVA aqueous can potentially be employed in various printing methods as an advanced anti-counterfeiting ink.

In summary, CDs were fabricated by ultrapure water as the only liquid phase reaction medium and chitosan as the source of C and N elements. The CO/CN was the luminescence center and the abundant –OH and –NH2 on the surface of CDs can reduce the energy gap between S1and T1, accelerate the ISC process and consequently produce more excited triplet excitons for RTP. By adjunction of the CDs into polymers, the bright blue fluorescence and green RTP can be observed from the CDs-polymers (except PEG). The RTP lifetime of CDs-PVA was 201 ms under 365 nm excitation, where the intermolecular hydrogen bonds played a key role to anchor the stimulated triplets and prevent the non-radiative transitions. Because of the FL and RTP properties of CDs-polymers, they can be applied as security ink in anti-counterfeiting. More interestingly, those intermolecular hydrogen bonds can be broken by water molecules can therefore the RTP of the samples was quenched immediately. Therefore, CDs-PVA was used to construct an RTP-test paper (RTP-TP) through a visualization and quantitative method to assay trace water in different organic environments. The RTP-TP was also examined in different commercial organic solvents. Compared to other biomass-CDs-based probes and metal materials, this research achieved equivalent or lower LODs (e.g., 0.013% in ethanol), satisfactory recovery (e.g., 101.5% in ethanol) and favorable stability. Therefore, our research opens a new door for construction visual RTP-TP based on non-aromatic biomass carbon dots and expands its application field.
The data that support the findings of this study are openly available in [figshare] at https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.14374595.

Yeyan Ni: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Pingyan Zhou: Formal analysis, Characterizations and analysis the data. Qiwen Jiang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Qi Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Xingyu Huang: Writing – review & editing, Writing and reviewing the original manuscript. Yi Jing: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, All the authors gave final approval of the version to be published and agreed to be accountable for all aspects of the work in questions related to its accuracy. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial assistance support for this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31370583).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109923.
